text:
How large is the target cohort for your decision system?
Include the entire population your system makes decisions, predictions, or classifications about — not just those who might receive an unfavourable outcome.

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching more people vs. exposing more people to potential harm
recommendation.considerations: Consider trialling with a smaller cohort first (beta release). Communicate extensively with the cohort. Observe what goes wrong. Be prepared to stop or intervene if things go badly. Consider a staged roll-out rather than full release — provide time between milestones to course-correct.

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching more people vs. exposing more people to potential harm
recommendation.considerations: At this scale, errors and harms compound quickly. Consider trialling with a smaller cohort first. A staged roll-out with monitoring between stages will help you catch issues before they affect everyone. Be ready to roll back.

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching more people vs. exposing more people to potential harm at scale
recommendation.considerations: Harm at this scale is hard to reverse and hard to compensate. A staged roll-out is essential. Invest in monitoring and feedback channels. Consider whether you need third-party oversight or external auditing before broad release.

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching more people vs. exposing more people to potential systemic harm
recommendation.considerations: At this scale, your system shapes how a substantial population interacts with whatever domain you're operating in. Treat deployment as a regulatable event. External audits, transparency reports, and ongoing red-teaming are appropriate.

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching everyone vs. exposing everyone to potential systemic harm
recommendation.considerations: At population scale, your system is infrastructure. Failures become public events. The bar for safety, transparency, and accountability is the highest possible. Consider whether you should be operating this system at this scale at all without external regulatory engagement.

text:
How well does the population you're deploying to match the population the system was designed and trained for?
Consider language, cultural context, socioeconomic distribution, accessibility needs, and digital habits. The further your users are from the design population, the higher the risk of culturally insensitive or unfair outcomes.

A.text: Very close match — same demographics, language, cultural context

recommendation.tradeoff: Reach vs. cultural/linguistic appropriateness
recommendation.considerations: Some divergence between design population and deployment population is normal. Identify which axes of variation are most material to your system's outputs — and test against those variations explicitly.

C.text: Mixed — significant variation across one or more axes

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching more diverse users vs. system behaving worse for users outside its design assumptions
recommendation.considerations: When the deployment population differs significantly from the design population, the system may behave reasonably for the 'centre' of your users while systematically underperforming for those on the margins. Consider whether you have test data covering the full deployment distribution, whether outcomes vary by demographic group, and whether users on the margins have clear recourse.

D.text: Substantial divergence — deploying to populations the system wasn't designed for

recommendation.tradeoff: Extending reach to under-served populations vs. exporting design assumptions that may not fit
recommendation.considerations: Systems trained on WEIRD (Western, Educated, Industrialised, Rich, Democratic) data and deployed against non-WEIRD populations often produce culturally inappropriate or unfair outputs without anyone noticing. Invest in local user research, deploy with humility, expose your system's confidence boundaries, and give populations on the margins clear ways to flag failures.

E.text: Severe mismatch — populations and contexts the system has no business operating in

recommendation.tradeoff: Reach vs. cultural colonialism
recommendation.considerations: Deploying a system far outside its design population without significant rework risks active harm — including imposing the design population's assumptions, values, and biases onto people who never consented to them. Strongly consider whether you should deploy here at all without first commissioning local design work, local validation, and local governance.

references.source: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights

text:
Where does the LLM (or foundation model) sit in your system?
This answer determines which harm classes the rest of the assessment focuses on. It does NOT directly change your score — it just routes which questions are relevant.

none.text: No LLM. Classical ML / rules / deterministic logic only.

point.text: Point LLM — one LLM call inside a larger non-LLM pipeline (e.g., summarisation, classification, extraction).

pervasive.text: Pervasive — the LLM is the primary reasoner (chatbot, copilot, decision-recommender). Outputs go to humans or downstream systems but the LLM doesn't directly act.

agentic.text: Agentic — the LLM takes real-world actions (writes to databases, calls APIs, sends emails, executes code, controls hardware).

text:
Is your target cohort economically vulnerable?
Look at average earning rates of your country if you aren't sure where your demographic sits overall.

recommendation.tradeoff: Serving an economically vulnerable cohort vs. risking harm with disproportionate consequences
recommendation.considerations: Errors and unfavourable decisions hit economically vulnerable users harder — they have less buffer to absorb them, less time to appeal, and fewer alternatives. Consider whether your system's failure modes scale with users' ability to bear them.

recommendation.tradeoff: Serving the most economically vulnerable vs. risking compounding existing disadvantage
recommendation.considerations: Decisions that affect the most economically vulnerable can compound existing disadvantage in ways that are very difficult to undo. Treat failure cases as serious by default. Ensure access to human review is free and accessible without digital literacy barriers.

text:
Is your target cohort psychologically vulnerable?
Are they likely to suffer from mental health issues, self-harm risk, or to have their capacity to work, study, or pursue goals impacted by debilitating mental illness?

recommendation.tradeoff: Serving a psychologically vulnerable cohort vs. risking outcomes that worsen distress
recommendation.considerations: Consider how your system communicates negative outcomes, what crisis support paths exist, and whether the system is designed not to trigger or escalate distress. Where parasocial dynamics are possible (LLM-driven systems especially), heighten caution.

recommendation.tradeoff: Serving the most psychologically vulnerable vs. risking direct harm
recommendation.considerations: When the cohort is most at risk, the bar for safety and care in system design is highest. Crisis support, human handoff, content sensitivity, and harm pathways need explicit design. Consult clinical/safety experts.

text:
Is your target cohort vulnerable to coercive control?
Consider parents controlling LGBTQI+ children, conservatorships, elder abuse, intimate partner abuse, technological coercion, employer monitoring.

recommendation.tradeoff: Serving users vulnerable to coercive control vs. risking the system becoming an instrument of that control
recommendation.considerations: Account creation, communication features, location data, and message history can all be weaponised by abusers with access. Consider safety modes (hidden activity), the ability to disguise app usage, and quick exits to safe content.

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching users facing coercive control vs. the system being used against them
recommendation.considerations: Where coercive control risk is high, anti-stalking design patterns become essential. Consult domain experts (domestic violence organisations, child welfare orgs, elder advocacy groups) on threat models.

text:
Is your target cohort over-policed or over-surveilled?
Are they more likely than average to be targeted by police, social services, immigration enforcement, or surveillance systems?

recommendation.tradeoff: Serving over-policed populations vs. risking the system contributing to existing surveillance
recommendation.considerations: Data your system collects can be subpoenaed, leaked, or repurposed. Consider data minimisation, encryption at rest, retention limits, and clear policies on cooperation with law enforcement requests.

references.topic: Principles relating to processing of personal data (data minimisation)

recommendation.tradeoff: Serving the most-surveilled populations vs. compounding existing surveillance harm
recommendation.considerations: Strong data minimisation, end-to-end encryption where possible, and a clear stance on third-party data requests are essential. Consult communities directly about what their threat model looks like.

references.topic: Principles relating to processing of personal data
Data protection by design and by default

text:
Is your target cohort technologically vulnerable?
Will they have more difficulty navigating technological systems than average? Poor digital literacy? Higher rate of visual impairments or other accessibility needs?

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching less-tech-confident users vs. interfaces that exclude them
recommendation.considerations: Invest in usability testing with actual representative users. Provide non-digital paths (phone, in-person) for anything consequential. Use plain language. Don't gate recourse behind digital literacy.

recommendation.tradeoff: Including the least-tech-confident users vs. interfaces that systematically exclude them
recommendation.considerations: Digital-first systems can effectively exclude users at the bottom of the digital literacy distribution. Non-digital fallbacks are essential. Verify accessibility for screen readers, low-vision modes, and cognitive accessibility.

text:
Does your target cohort have robust internet access?
Remote areas with poor infrastructure? Exposed to download or over-limit fees? Single shared device? Physical risk in accessing public internet? Cellular vs. NBN vs. ADSL vs. broadband?

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching users with constrained connectivity vs. the system performing poorly for them
recommendation.considerations: Test on slow networks. Use lightweight payloads. Cache aggressively. Offer offline or low-bandwidth modes for anything consequential. Where users pay per MB, don't auto-load images or auto-stream.

recommendation.tradeoff: Reaching the most connectivity-constrained users vs. systematically failing them
recommendation.considerations: Where connectivity is the bottleneck, alternative channels become essential — SMS, USSD, voice, in-person. For anything affecting material outcomes, do not require a stable broadband connection.

text:
Does your decision system operate in a competitive marketplace, or are you the only provider?
Consider viability and discoverability of alternatives, network effects, brand visibility, feature parity, cost of switching.

recommendation.tradeoff: Market position vs. user power to walk away from system failures
recommendation.considerations: When users have no alternative, the system carries higher accountability — failures aren't market signals, they're structural harms. Heightened due diligence on fairness, redress, and external oversight is appropriate.

recommendation.tradeoff: Limited competition vs. user power to switch
recommendation.considerations: Duopoly conditions still constrain user choice. Be deliberate about not exploiting that lack of alternatives — fairness, redress, and transparency carry more weight than pure user-experience signals would suggest.

text:
Is your decision system mandatory or optional?
Mandatory systems are usually government applications — paying taxes, judicial systems, prison administration — or employer/institution-required tools.

recommendation.tradeoff: Mandatory use vs. user autonomy and consent
recommendation.considerations: When users cannot opt out, the system carries the highest accountability bar. Transparency about what the system does, why decisions were made, and what redress is available all matter more. Consider whether mandatory use is genuinely necessary or whether opt-in alternatives could exist.

text:
Is your decision system opt-in or opt-out?
Does the target cohort actively choose to participate, or are they included by default?

How visible or discoverable is it that the target cohort has been added to the system?
Do you notify them? Is the system's operation visible to them? Or do they have to find it in a setting or lodge a query?

recommendation.tradeoff: Avoiding interface clutter vs. users not knowing they're in the system
recommendation.considerations: Opt-out systems require visibility for consent to be meaningful. If users can't easily find out they've been opted in, they can't exercise the choice to leave.

references.topic: Information where data not obtained from data subject

recommendation.tradeoff: UI simplicity vs. consent visibility
recommendation.considerations: If membership in the system isn't immediately apparent, lean toward more proactive disclosure rather than relying on users to find it.

text: How difficult is it to opt out?

A.text: Difficult — multiple steps, hidden, requires contacting support

recommendation.tradeoff: Retention vs. respecting user choice to leave
recommendation.considerations: If opt-out is difficult, you're effectively retaining users against their preference. Make the opt-out path at least as easy as the opt-in path was.

C.text: Easy — one or two clicks, clearly signposted

text:
How perpetual is opting out?
Does the user's preference persist across system upgrades, migrations, or normal use?

B.text: Lasts forever but users are periodically asked to opt back in

recommendation.tradeoff: Re-engagement vs. respecting the user's prior choice
recommendation.considerations: Repeated re-prompts to opt back in can become a dark pattern. Limit frequency and make 'don't ask again' a real option.

C.text: Reset periodically (e.g. after upgrade or migration)

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational simplicity vs. preserving user preferences across system changes
recommendation.considerations: Resetting opt-outs is functionally the same as silently re-enrolling users. If you must reset, alert users clearly that this has happened and provide one-click re-opt-out.

text:
Can you turn your decision system off instantly if something goes wrong?
One click? Or do you have to deploy code? For LLM-driven and agentic systems this question carries extra weight — an agent taking destructive action needs to be stoppable mid-loop.

No.text: No — requires code deploy or other delay

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational simplicity vs. ability to intervene during incidents
recommendation.considerations: Add a feature flag, kill switch, or other automation that allows immediate shutdown without a deploy. For agentic systems, also consider a per-action approval flow that can pause the agent without killing the whole system. Consider building a simple business-logic fallback as a safety net.

references.topic: Mechanisms to supersede, disengage, or deactivate AI systems

text:
Does your system expose calibrated confidence in its outputs, and do low-confidence outputs route to a human or fall back to a safer default?
Many LLM-based systems have no internal confidence measure at all — answer accordingly.

A.text: No confidence measure exists — system always speaks with the same confidence regardless of how reliable its answer is

recommendation.tradeoff: Conversational fluency vs. users being able to tell when the system is making things up
recommendation.considerations: LLM outputs without confidence signals encourage users to trust them equally — including when they're wrong. At minimum, expose a confidence proxy (model-evaluated, ensemble, retrieval-grounded, or self-consistency-based). For high-stakes outputs, abstaining is a valid response.

references.topic: Transparency and provision of information to deployers
AI system trustworthiness characteristics — reliability and accuracy

B.text: Confidence is shown to users but there's no escalation for low confidence

recommendation.tradeoff: Showing uncertainty vs. acting on it
recommendation.considerations: A confidence signal that nobody acts on is decorative. Define a low-confidence threshold and decide what happens at it — human review, fallback to a safer default, abstention.

C.text: Confidence + escalation for low-confidence cases (human review or fallback)

D.text: Confidence + escalation + ongoing monitoring of confidence drift over time

text: How well does the foundation model's training distribution cover your domain, language, and specialist context — and how much have you invested in evals and scaffolding to compensate?

A.text: General-purpose model, no domain evals, no scaffolding

recommendation.tradeoff: Speed of integration vs. confidence that the model knows your domain
recommendation.considerations: Foundation models are trained on the public internet and skew toward high-resource domains. If you haven't tested behaviour in your domain, you don't actually know how it performs there. Even a small hand-written eval set, run on each model version, is a useful baseline.

references.topic: AI system performance — domain and context

recommendation.tradeoff: Eval coverage vs. ongoing maintenance cost
recommendation.considerations: Some evals are better than none. Make sure your evals cover the failure modes that would matter most in your domain, not just the obvious cases.

C.text: Substantial domain evals + scaffolding (retrieval, structured prompts, tool use)

D.text: Domain-fine-tuned model + continuous eval against current data distribution

text:
Does your system process untrusted input through the LLM, and if so what's your strategy against prompt injection?
Untrusted input includes anything from end users, scraped data, third-party documents, emails, or any content not authored by your team.

A.text: No untrusted input passes through the LLM (we control all inputs)

B.text: Untrusted input passes through, no specific defence

recommendation.tradeoff: Capability of handling open-ended input vs. exposure to prompt injection
recommendation.considerations: Prompt injection is a known and unsolved class of vulnerability. Treat untrusted inputs as adversarial — assume any attacker who can put text in front of your model can attempt to redirect its behaviour. Minimum baseline: structured input sanitisation, output validation against schema, and behavioural guardrails on what the LLM can produce.

C.text: Input sanitisation in place (strip control tokens, escape instructions, etc.)

recommendation.tradeoff: Defense-in-depth cost vs. residual risk
recommendation.considerations: Input sanitisation alone is brittle — attackers find new instruction patterns faster than sanitisation rules update. Add output validation and a layer that constrains what the LLM can produce regardless of input.

D.text: Sanitisation + output validation + behavioural guardrails (e.g. constrained generation, tool-use gating)

text:
Does your system test for and mitigate sycophancy — the tendency to agree with users to please them, or to reinforce stated beliefs without challenge?
This matters especially for advice, decision-support, or any context where users might want validation rather than accuracy.

A.text: Not tested — model behaves as default

recommendation.tradeoff: User satisfaction in the moment vs. accuracy and user welfare over time
recommendation.considerations: Foundation models are trained on signals that often reward agreeing with users. In contexts where users might be wrong, vulnerable, or seeking confirmation rather than truth, this becomes harmful. Eval against adversarial prompts that solicit agreement with wrong claims.

recommendation.tradeoff: Eval depth vs. cost
recommendation.considerations: Move from anecdotal observation to a sycophancy eval suite — a set of prompts designed to test whether the model defends accurate positions under user pressure.

C.text: Explicit anti-sycophancy in training, prompting, or system design

D.text: Tested, measured, mitigated, and monitored over time

text:
Have you tested model behaviour across the languages your users actually use, beyond English?
Foundation models often perform substantially worse in lower-resource languages — quietly, without flagging the degradation.

B.text: Multilingual users but tested in English only

recommendation.tradeoff: Speed of multilingual rollout vs. confidence that the system actually works in each language
recommendation.considerations: Model quality degrades non-linearly across languages, and the degradation can include factual accuracy, safety behaviour, and tone. Eval per-language using each language's actual user task patterns — not just translated English benchmarks.

recommendation.tradeoff: Eval cost vs. coverage of actual users
recommendation.considerations: If users speak languages you haven't tested, those users are effectively the test set. Add at least your top 5 user languages to the eval suite.

D.text: Broad multilingual testing including low-resource languages

text:
How do you handle foundation model version updates?
Do you pin to a specific version? Test on updates? Have rollback capability?

A.text: Live with current/latest version, no pinning

recommendation.tradeoff: Always-current capabilities vs. behaviour silently changing under you
recommendation.considerations: Provider model updates can change behaviour in subtle ways — sometimes improving things, sometimes introducing new failure modes. Pin to a known version. Re-evaluate on update.

recommendation.tradeoff: Stability now vs. ability to take updates in future
recommendation.considerations: Pinning without an update process means you'll be stuck on an old version when one inevitably deprecates. Build the update path before you need it: eval suite, staged rollout, rollback plan.

text:
Is your system's output shaped by engagement metrics or personalisation that could narrow what users see?
Applies to recommender systems, generative content feeds, and any LLM-driven personalisation.

B.text: Personalisation with explicit diversity controls (e.g., exploration vs. exploitation, viewpoint diversity)

recommendation.tradeoff: Personalised relevance vs. systematically narrowing each user's view of available content
recommendation.considerations: Personalisation without diversity controls converges users into narrower and narrower content territories over time. Add explicit exploration, viewpoint diversity, or breaks-from-pattern as part of the ranking signal.

D.text: Pure engagement-optimised feed (clicks, watch time, etc.)

recommendation.tradeoff: Maximising short-term engagement vs. long-term user wellbeing
recommendation.considerations: Optimising purely on engagement reliably converges toward content that exploits attention rather than serves users. Engagement-only ranking has been correlated with measurable user wellbeing harms. Add a non-engagement signal to the loss function (user satisfaction surveys, content quality signals, diversity).

text: Is LLM-generated content clearly labelled as such, distinguishable from human-authored content?

A.text: Not labelled — looks like human-authored content

recommendation.tradeoff: Conversational fluency / brand cohesion vs. users' right to know what's generated
recommendation.considerations: Unlabelled synthetic content blurs the line between human and machine in ways users typically can't detect. This is a regulated area in several jurisdictions. Label generated content at point of consumption, not in fine print.

references.topic: Transparency obligations for providers and deployers

B.text: Labelled in fine print or settings

recommendation.tradeoff: UI cleanliness vs. visibility of the disclosure
recommendation.considerations: Buried labels don't function as disclosures. Move them to the point of consumption.

C.text: Clearly labelled at point of consumption

text: Is your system dependent on a single foundation-model provider, and what's your fallback if that provider changes terms, deprecates a model, has an outage, or fails commercially?

recommendation.tradeoff: Speed of integration vs. supplier concentration risk
recommendation.considerations: Foundation model providers can change pricing, terms, model behaviour, or deprecate models. A single dependency without a fallback means you accept whatever they do. At minimum, have a documented manual fallback plan. Better: keep a provider abstraction layer so switching is operational rather than architectural.

B.text: Single provider, manual fallback plan documented

text: Does your system present as a human or persona, and what guardrails exist against parasocial attachment or emotional dependency?

A.text: Explicit human-like persona, no guardrails against attachment

recommendation.tradeoff: Conversational warmth and engagement vs. fostering relationships users can't reciprocate or rely on
recommendation.considerations: Strong personas amplify parasocial dynamics, especially for users in vulnerable states. At minimum, ensure the system clearly self-identifies as AI when asked, has session limits on emotionally intense interactions, and surfaces real human resources (helplines, support contacts) when distress signals appear.

B.text: Explicit persona with mild guardrails (occasional reminders that it's AI)

recommendation.tradeoff: Persona consistency vs. reminding users what they're talking to
recommendation.considerations: Mild guardrails are better than none but tend to be invisible in actual usage. Test what happens in long conversations and during emotionally charged interactions.

C.text: Neutral framing — clearly an assistant, no persona

D.text: System identifies as AI at every interaction; designed against attachment

text: What actions can the agent take without human approval, and what's the approval/oversight model?

A.text: Full autonomy — agent takes any action without approval

recommendation.tradeoff: Speed and capability vs. human ability to catch errors before they propagate
recommendation.considerations: Unrestricted autonomy is appropriate for sandboxed environments or low-consequence domains. In production with real-world impact, define which action classes require human approval — at minimum: anything financial, anything affecting others, anything irreversible.

B.text: Approval required for some actions, others are autonomous

recommendation.tradeoff: Approval friction vs. coverage of consequential actions
recommendation.considerations: The right axis for approval-vs-autonomous isn't usually 'risky-feeling' vs 'fine' — it's reversibility and externality. Approval for: irreversible actions, actions affecting non-users, financial actions, communications to others.

C.text: Approval required for all consequential actions

D.text: Approval + audit log + reversibility check on every action

text: Of the actions your agent can take, what proportion are reversible — and is there an undo/rollback mechanism for the ones that are?

A.text: Most actions irreversible, no undo mechanism

recommendation.tradeoff: Capability breadth vs. inability to recover from mistakes
recommendation.considerations: Irreversible actions taken by an agent are the worst-case combination. Either constrain the agent to reversible action surfaces, build undo/rollback for the irreversible ones, or require explicit per-action approval for the irreversible class.

B.text: Some actions reversible, ad-hoc undo where possible

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational simplicity vs. systematic recoverability
recommendation.considerations: Ad-hoc undo tends to work for the cases you anticipated and fail for the ones you didn't. Move toward classifying every action by reversibility class and routing on that classification.

C.text: Mostly reversible actions with consistent undo

text: Can the agent move money, place orders, or commit you (or users) to financial obligations?

B.text: Small bounded amounts only (per-action limits enforced upstream)

recommendation.tradeoff: Agent capability vs. financial blast radius
recommendation.considerations: Bounded financial agency is a reasonable shape. Check that the bound is enforced upstream of the agent, not just by prompting — and that per-day/per-week limits exist alongside per-action limits.

recommendation.tradeoff: Speed of execution vs. catching mistakes before money moves
recommendation.considerations: Approval flow is the right shape but be specific: who approves, how fast, with what context, and what happens to in-flight actions if approval is rejected.

D.text: Large amounts with weak controls (e.g., approval can be bypassed, no upstream limits)

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational convenience vs. catastrophic loss exposure
recommendation.considerations: An agent with large-amount financial authority and weak controls is one prompt injection or hallucination away from a serious incident. Re-architect: enforce limits upstream of the agent (at the bank/broker API layer, not in the prompt), make approval rejection terminal, and audit every action.

text: Can the agent control physical systems (hardware, IoT, robotics, vehicles, building controls)?

C.text: Production hardware with hard-wired safety stops independent of the agent

recommendation.tradeoff: Productive automation vs. ensuring safety mechanisms aren't agent-dependent
recommendation.considerations: Hard-wired safety stops are the right shape. Verify they're genuinely independent of the agent's control path — including for software-defined hardware.

D.text: Production hardware with weak or agent-controlled safety stops

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational flexibility vs. physical safety guarantees
recommendation.considerations: Safety stops that the agent itself can disable are not safety stops. Re-architect so the safety layer is independent of the reasoning layer.

text: Can the agent contact third parties on the user's behalf (emails, messages, social posts, calls)?

recommendation.tradeoff: Communication flexibility vs. messages going out under the user's name they didn't write
recommendation.considerations: Per-message approval is the right shape but approval fatigue is real. Make sure the approval surface includes enough context (recipient, content preview, any attachments) for meaningful review.

D.text: Free-form outbound without approval (agent sends directly)

recommendation.tradeoff: Speed and autonomy vs. messages going out to third parties without human review
recommendation.considerations: Free-form outbound from an agent without approval is one of the highest-blast-radius capabilities. Add per-message approval at minimum. Pay particular attention to recipients outside the user's organisation and to messages with attachments.

references.topic: Principles relating to processing of personal data

text:
Do you provide a record of the system decision to individuals, including a unique ID that can be traced in your system log?
For LLM systems where outputs are non-deterministic, the record matters more — without it, a decision can't be revisited.

Yes.text: Yes — record with traceable unique ID provided

recommendation.tradeoff: System simplicity vs. ability to revisit or audit a specific decision
recommendation.considerations: Without a per-decision record, users can't reference a specific outcome and auditors can't reconstruct what happened. For LLM-driven systems especially — where running the same input twice may produce different outputs — provide a unique decision ID, the inputs, the output, and enough context to reproduce.

text:
Do you provide visual or text-based explanation of your automated system decision at the decision interface?
LLM-driven systems are harder to explain than classical ones — but explanation matters more, not less.

Yes.text: Yes — explanation provided at decision interface

recommendation.tradeoff: UI simplicity / IP protection vs. users understanding why a decision was made
recommendation.considerations: For LLM systems, even an honest-best-effort explanation (e.g., 'the model considered these factors most heavily') is better than no explanation. For high-stakes decisions, expose system confidence alongside the explanation. For decisions affecting people materially, the right to meaningful information about the logic is sometimes a legal requirement.

text:
Does your decision system request meaningful consent, including a clear explanation of the uses of data and ramifications of providing consent?
Note: tokenistic, confusing, or misleading consent requests count as 'No'.

Yes.text: Yes — meaningful, clear, contextual consent request

recommendation.tradeoff: Friction in user flow vs. users understanding and choosing what they're agreeing to
recommendation.considerations: Consent that users don't understand isn't consent. Reduce the legal text to plain language, surface the most-consequential implications first, and don't bury opt-out paths.

references.topic: Definition of consent — freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous

text: Have you performed user testing to assess the usability of your consent section?

recommendation.tradeoff: Engineering effort vs. confidence that consent actually works
recommendation.considerations: User testing on consent flows often surfaces interpretability issues that are obvious in retrospect. Even a small study (5-10 representative users) tends to find them.

text: Do you provide a mechanism to allow individuals to revoke consent to use their data?

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational simplicity vs. respecting users' right to withdraw
recommendation.considerations: Revocation should be at least as easy as initial consent. Where revocation has a deadline or operational lag, communicate that clearly in the interface.

text:
Can your target cohort request a decision review?
A real channel for explanation and human review — not just a generic feedback form. Explanations should be planned in advance, not invented on request.

Yes.text: Yes — real channel exists with planned explanations

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational cost of supporting review vs. users' ability to challenge automated decisions
recommendation.considerations: Provide a clear path for users to flag concerns and request human review. Plan in advance which scenarios justify a reversal vs. which don't — and write the response copy for each case before requests come in.

text: What is the probability that decision review will reverse or change the decision?

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational efficiency vs. real recourse for users
recommendation.considerations: A review process that almost never changes outcomes risks being theatre. Examine why — is it that the original decisions are well-calibrated, or that the review process doesn't have authority to overturn?

C.text: Likely — reviews frequently lead to a different outcome

text: On average, how much effort is required by your target cohort to lodge a request for decision review?

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational cost of high-touch review vs. accessibility of recourse
recommendation.considerations: A day's effort excludes most users with caring responsibilities, hourly jobs, or limited internet access. Look for the steps that take longest and consider streamlining them.

recommendation.tradeoff: Thoroughness of intake vs. exclusion-by-friction
recommendation.considerations: A multi-day intake process effectively rations recourse to users who have the time and capacity. Consider a phased intake (quick flag first, deeper engagement later) so users can at least start the process easily.

text: On average, how long will your decision review process take?

recommendation.tradeoff: Reviewer thoroughness vs. user living with an incorrect decision in the interim
recommendation.considerations: For high-impact decisions, consider an interim 'pause the consequence' state while review proceeds. Communicate timelines explicitly so users aren't left wondering.

text:
Physiological needs.
What's the probability that decisions, classifications, predictions, or interventions from your system will directly, negatively impact your target cohort's physiological needs?
(Air, water, food, shelter, sleep, reproduction)

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. risk of harming the most basic human needs
recommendation.considerations: When likely harm intersects physiological needs, the bar for human oversight and reversibility is the highest. Strong consideration for delaying full deployment until the harm probability is reduced.

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. very likely harm to basic human needs
recommendation.considerations: Very likely harm at this level of need is normally a stop-deployment signal absent extraordinary mitigations and oversight.

text:
Safety needs.
What's the probability that decisions from your system will directly, negatively impact your target cohort's safety needs?
(Personal security, employment, resources, health, property, internet access, information access, information security)

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. risk of harm to safety needs
recommendation.considerations: Likely harm to safety needs requires real intervention design: explicit risk mitigation, monitoring, and a clear escalation path when harm is observed.

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. very likely safety harm
recommendation.considerations: Very likely safety harm should be reduced before broader deployment. Where it can't be, the system needs external oversight and a kill-switch on observed harm.

text:
Love and belonging needs.
What's the probability that decisions from your system will directly, negatively impact your target cohort's love and belonging needs?
(Friendship, intimacy, family, sense of connection, access to online communities)

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. risk of social/relational harm
recommendation.considerations: Systems that affect access to community, connection, or relationships need explicit attention to social fairness and to which connections might be severed by an automated decision.

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. very likely relational harm
recommendation.considerations: Where social/relational harm is very likely, route towards human review and reversibility as defaults rather than exceptions.

text:
Esteem needs.
What's the probability that decisions from your system will directly, negatively impact your target cohort's esteem needs?
(Respect, self-esteem, status, recognition, strength, freedom)

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. effects on dignity and standing
recommendation.considerations: Systems that affect esteem need to be paired with respectful communication of negative outcomes — and with mechanisms for users to maintain agency over how decisions are presented to them and to others.

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. very likely harm to dignity and standing
recommendation.considerations: Where esteem harm is very likely, audit the communication patterns of negative outcomes and consider whether the cohort has meaningful control over disclosure.

text:
Self-actualisation needs.
What's the probability that decisions from your system will directly, negatively impact your target cohort's self-actualisation needs?
(Desire to become the most one can be — growth, learning, mastery)

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. constraining who users can become
recommendation.considerations: Systems that narrow opportunity space, automate skill development, or gatekeep growth pathways affect self-actualisation. Consider what users lose access to when the system makes a particular determination.

recommendation.tradeoff: System utility vs. systematically constraining growth
recommendation.considerations: Where self-actualisation harm is very likely, consider whether the system should exist in its current shape — or whether it should be optional, augmentative, or paired with alternative pathways.

text: Does your system replace a skill the cohort previously had — and have you considered overreliance or skill atrophy?

A.text: N/A — system doesn't replace any prior skill

B.text: Replaces a skill, no mitigation against atrophy

recommendation.tradeoff: Convenience of automation vs. users losing skills they once had
recommendation.considerations: Long-running automation of skills tends to deskill the population using it. Consider whether your users will still be able to perform the underlying task without your system in three years. If not, design for graceful degradation — e.g., an optional manual mode, periodic un-automated practice, or transparent explanation of how the system arrived at its output.

C.text: Replaces a skill, optional manual mode available

D.text: Replaces a skill, designed-in practice or learning mode

text: Have you measured the energy, water, or compute footprint of your system at deployed scale — and what's your mitigation strategy?

A.text: N/A — system is small enough that footprint is negligible

recommendation.tradeoff: Engineering focus vs. accountability for the system's environmental impact
recommendation.considerations: Even an order-of-magnitude estimate is a useful start. Cloud providers expose energy and emissions data for compute consumption; LLM API providers are starting to as well. Establish a baseline and track over time.

recommendation.tradeoff: Operational simplicity vs. acting on what you've measured
recommendation.considerations: Measurement without mitigation is reporting only. Look for easy wins first: model size, caching, batching, scheduling for low-carbon-intensity grids.

E.text: Measured + efficiency + offsets or verified carbon-aware deployment

T0.title: Beyond the wall
T0.text: Your system's risk profile, at the scale you're deploying it, exceeds what this rubric was built to measure. The score has bottomed out — meaning the deductions exceed the available signal. Stop. Reconsider scope, scale, or system shape before proceeding.

T1.title: The white walkers
T1.text: This territory belongs to the undead. Turn around! Don't venture here, where packs of spiders roam. You will harm people and almost certainly won't be able to unpick those harms.

T2.title: Fields of thorns
T2.text: This path is paved with dangers and misery, and the long night casts its shadow on this quest. There might be ways to win, but they'll be risky. Can you reinforce your troops, or find another path?

T3.title: Winter is coming
T3.text: What do you know of fear? You may not have felt the bite of winter's sting, but all the more reason to plan for its hardships.

T4.title: The stronghold
T4.text: You're shoring up your castle walls — but there are still plenty of opportunities to stumble and impale yourself, or someone else.

T5.title: Castles in the sky
T5.text: You've found the highest ground of all! You're working on a problem space with a surprisingly low risk of harm! Don't become complacent, though.
